topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the familiar lines of the worn paperback on his lap, the scent of aged paper suddenly acrid. He’d devoured Alistair Finch’s work for years, finding solace in the intricate world-building and the heartbreakingly nuanced characters. Then, the discovery hit him—a passage, a distinct sequence of metaphors, lifted almost verbatim from his own unpublished manuscript, now appearing in Finch’s latest bestseller. Elias felt a cold knot tighten in his stomach, his fingers gripping the spine until his knuckles were white. He stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "This is impossible," he muttered, his voice tight with disbelief. He paced the small apartment, running a hand through his already dishevelled hair, the air growing thick and suffocating. Every word Finch had written now seemed coated in a layer of bitter falsehood.

[story 2]

"You couldn't just steal that?" Sarah demanded, her voice dangerously low as she stared at the printed page. The ink seemed to mock her. She reread the section, her breath catching in her throat. It was undeniably hers—her unique narrative voice, the very rhythm she had painstakingly crafted over months of solitude. A flush crept up her neck, spreading across her cheeks. She slammed the book shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet study. Her hands began to tremble, a frantic, restless energy bubbling beneath her skin. She leaned back in her chair, staring blankly at the wall, the silence amplifying the churning frustration in her chest. She needed to know who had done this, needed to confront the audacity of the theft.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the screen, the glowing text mocking him with its familiar syntax. The familiar cadence of his prose, the specific emotional arc he had labored over, was laid bare, repurposed by someone else. A wave of profound unease washed over him, making his vision swim slightly. He slowly lowered the tablet, his shoulders slumping as if the weight of the revelation had physically crushed him. He walked to the window, pulling the heavy curtain aside, letting the harsh afternoon light flood the room. He felt a sudden, sharp surge of heat behind his eyes, a burning resentment that made his jaw clench so hard it ached. He turned back, his expression hardening into something utterly resolute, a quiet fury settling deep within his core.
